name: Riboflavin (Vitamin B2)
contraindications: No major contraindications at nutritional intakes were highlighted in this source set

sources.title: Riboflavin Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

description: Riboflavin is required to form FMN and FAD coenzymes used in energy and antioxidant pathways. The corpus distinguishes deficiency prevention at low intake levels from higher-dose migraine prophylaxis protocols studied in selected trials.
summary: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) is an essential vitamin cofactor for cellular redox metabolism with a strong safety profile; migraine protocols use pharmacologic doses that are distinct from dietary intake levels.
mechanism: Proposed/established mix: riboflavin supports redox reactions through FMN/FAD and may improve mitochondrial energy handling in migraine contexts; migraine-specific mechanisms remain proposed rather than definitive.
safetyNotes: Riboflavin is generally very well tolerated, including at high supplemental doses in published protocols.
Bright yellow urine discoloration is common and typically benign.
interactions: No major clinically significant drug interactions are emphasized in the source corpus.
dosage: Migraine research protocols commonly use 400 mg/day, which is a pharmacologic dose rather than a dietary-intake target.
preparation: Available as stand-alone tablets/capsules and in B-complex products; formulations are typically kept light-protected.
regulatoryStatus: Essential vitamin with established dietary reference intakes; marketed as a standard dietary supplement ingredient.
